acceptance_targets: [CRAFT_OPERATIONAL_LEGIBILITY_BREACH] In the revised breach passage anchored at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:137; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:141; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:145; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:149, a cold reader can state what tool each person is using, what part of the grate or bolt it engages, what observable change occurs, and why the method plausibly almost works but fails. If the reader still has to invent how a pipe turns a recessed hex bolt, this finding is not resolved.

[CRAFT_FALSE_FAILURE_DIAGNOSIS] After revision, a reader comparing the prep scene at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:19; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:25; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:29; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:79 with the aftermath at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:215; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:223 should get the same answer to 'why did the raid fail?' If the coda still claims they never asked about the door system, this finding fails.

Fail if four confessions still arrive in the same shape and cadence across lines 2311-2353.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised breach passage anchored at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:137; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:141; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:145; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:149, a cold reader can state what tool each person is using, what part of the grate or bolt it engages, what observable change occurs, and why the method plausibly almost works but fails. If the reader still has to invent how a pipe turns a recessed hex bolt, this finding is not resolved.
findings.problem: The chapter's decisive physical process is not mechanically legible. Marcus identifies recessed hex bolts, but the raid arrives with a pipe and shelving bracket rather than a tool that can credibly engage recessed hex heads; the prose then asks the reader to believe those improvised tools produce quarter-turns and a meaningful shift in a corroded industrial grate. Because the near-success is the hinge of the set piece, the unclear hardware interaction makes the scene feel plot-managed rather than earned.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the breach mechanics at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:44; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:137; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:141; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:145; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:149 so the page shows a believable method. Either give the team a tool that actually fits the recessed fasteners, or make brute-force prying/bending/lifting the operative plan and describe exactly what metal gives, what does not, and why the grate opens only an inch before the defenders arrive.

findings.acceptance_test: After revision, a reader comparing the prep scene at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:19; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:25; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:29; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:79 with the aftermath at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:215; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:223 should get the same answer to 'why did the raid fail?' If the coda still claims they never asked about the door system, this finding fails.
findings.problem: The coda misstates the chapter's own causality. Noor explicitly asks Marcus about the mechanical-room door, learns it may be a different credential system, and frames the grate as the real plan; later, the summary says the raid was built on 'a door system nobody thought to ask about.' That contradiction muddies why the assault actually fails and makes Noor's self-indictment sound imposed rather than earned from the staged action.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the failure diagnosis at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:215, with supporting cleanup if needed at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:213 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:223, so Noor's after-action account matches the prep at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:19; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:25; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:29; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:79; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:133. If she blames herself, anchor that blame to causes the chapter actually dramatizes: defended territory, inadequate breach method, or overestimating what Marcus's incomplete intel could buy.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the pre-assault scene no longer features four structurally identical [confession + pause + acceptance] turns in sequence, and at least two characters' responses break the template through compression, oblique delivery, silence, or absence of a clean rhetorical landing. Fail if four confessions still arrive in the same shape and cadence across lines 2311-2353.

evidence.excerpt: 2311: "I know what the switch means." Upright now, he gets some of his old institutional height back, but there's nothing under it anymore. "Let them send it all. The embezzlement. All of it. My name is on the release packet for version three. I read the warnings and still helped wave it through. Every person in this building is here because people like me kept calling it acceptable." He adjusts his glasses. "I owe them a floor grate. At minimum."

2343: "Nine days ago that was the reason nobody tried this," Noor says. "Nine days ago we thought two weeks would end and we'd go home with our secrets intact. Fifteen people are dead. Let them print the fabrication cover-up. Let my chair read it. I don't care. I care about the server rack."
2347: Samir looks at the floor. Then he looks up. He doesn't whisper this time. "I sent money home through channels that weren't — they weren't legal. It's in my file." A pause. "Let them see it."
2351: James snaps the rubber band twice. "My daughter's name is in my file. Not mine. Hers. She's seven." He swallows. "But yeah. Fine. Let them send it."

My name is on the release packet for version three. I read the warnings and still helped wave it through. Every person in this building is here because people like me kept calling it acceptable." He adjusts his glasses. "I owe them a floor grate. At minimum."
2312: 
2313: Guilt isn't currency and dying in a sublevel doesn't fix a safety certification, but Noor doesn't say it. Noor nods, because he's coming and she needs him and the reasons don't matter anymore.
2314:

2352:

findings.problem: Four characters (Viktor, Noor, Samir, James) deliver pre-assault confessions in structurally identical [confession + pause + acceptance] turns across lines 2311-2353. Each gets a beat to declare willingness to accept the dead man's switch consequences: Viktor confesses embezzlement and says 'I owe them a floor grate' (2311); Noor says 'Let them print the fabrication cover-up' (2343); Samir says 'I sent money home through channels that weren't legal... Let them see it' (2347); James says 'My daughter's name is in my file... But yeah. Fine. Let them send it' (2351). The round-robin creates a liturgical cadence that reduces each character's specific moral pressure to a slot in a template. Viktor's confession lands because it is first; by the fourth, the reader can predict the shape.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2311-2353. Break the four-person symmetry. Options: (a) Collapse two of the mid-list confessions into a single compressed beat — a nod, a gesture, a line that covers two people at once — saving dramatic weight for the confession that matters most to the scene's emotional architecture. (b) Let one character refuse to confess or confess obliquely, forcing the group to proceed without clean consensus. (c) Let one character's acceptance arrive through action rather than the confession-pause-acceptance speech template. Helen's silence (line 2349) already breaks the template effectively; extend that principle to at least one more character. Rachel's 'We going?' (line 2353) is also good. The goal is to prevent the scene from reading as a liturgical round where each person deposits their sin.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if line 2407 no longer opens with 'The sound is [adjective(s)]' and uses a construction specific to the loading-dock setting and weapon. The wet-grain simile may remain. Fail if the opening clause still follows the formula or recycles 'dense' or 'wet' from other chapters.

evidence.excerpt: 2407: The sound is dense and wet. It isn't the crack Noor expected. It's heavier, like a bag of wet grain dropped onto pavement. Viktor goes forward onto his face. His glasses skitter across the dock floor and come to rest near the compressor housing, one lens down. The bracket clatters out of his hands. His body hits the concrete without any attempt to break the fall. No arms out, no turning, just the weight of him meeting the floor.

rewrite_direction.excerpt: 2407: The sound is dense and wet. It isn't the crack Noor expected. It's heavier, like a bag of wet grain dropped onto pavement. Viktor goes forward onto his face. His glasses skitter across the dock floor and come to rest near the compressor housing, one lens down. The bracket clatters out of his hands. His body hits the concrete without any attempt to break the fall. No arms out, no turning, just the weight of him meeting the floor.

findings.problem: Line 2407: 'The sound is dense and wet.' Fifth instance, and the most formulaic — 'dense and wet' directly inverts Chapter 2's 'wet and dense.' Viktor's death on the loading dock is the novel's most important secondary-character death and it deserves its own sentence rather than a recycled construction.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2407. Viktor is struck by a fire extinguisher on a concrete dock floor — the material (steel on skull on concrete), the outdoor-adjacent acoustics, and Viktor's specific physical stature provide ample material for a unique construction. The simile that follows ('like a bag of wet grain dropped onto pavement') is strong and specific; pair it with an equally specific opening rather than the template.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if line 2363 uses at most one sensory detail for the building's smell rather than the full sweet-chemical-copper taxonomy. Fail if the passage still assembles three or more named components of the decomposition sensory package into a catalogue.

evidence.excerpt: 2363: The corridor smells like meat that isn't food. Day eleven, and decomposition has its own gradient now: the heavy sweetness of tissue breakdown, the sharper chemical edge of gases, and under it the specific copper smell that means someone bled out in a confined space. Noor breathes through her mouth and keeps moving.

rewrite_direction.excerpt: 2363: The corridor smells like meat that isn't food. Day eleven, and decomposition has its own gradient now: the heavy sweetness of tissue breakdown, the sharper chemical edge of gases, and under it the specific copper smell that means someone bled out in a confined space. Noor breathes through her mouth and keeps moving.

findings.problem: Line 2363: 'the heavy sweetness of tissue breakdown, the sharper chemical edge of gases, and under it the specific copper smell that means someone bled out in a confined space.' This is the most fully elaborated version of the decomposition taxonomy. Every component (sweet tissue breakdown, chemical gas edge, copper blood) has appeared individually in chapters 5-11. Here they are assembled into a complete three-part catalogue that reads like a style-bible entry deployed in full. This appears during the assault sequence, where pacing should be at its tightest.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2363. Replace the three-part decomposition taxonomy with a single fresh sensory detail specific to this corridor during the assault — something Noor notices because she is moving fast and afraid, not because the narrator is inventorying the air. 'Noor breathes through her mouth and keeps moving' (end of line 2363) is the right beat; cut everything before it down to one clause.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch 12 contains at most 2 atmospheric-sound descriptions and no passages that explicitly narrate the building's ambiance as 'unchanged' or 'the same.'
findings.evidence: 'fluorescent' appears 3 times in Ch 12 (lines 2253, 2361, 2435); atmospheric sounds appear 6 times. Combined: 9 atmospheric instances in 108 prose lines (8.3%). Line 2253: 'watches the fluorescent light hum its unchanged hum'; line 2435: 'the fluorescent light is still humming and nothing up here has changed at all.'
findings.problem: Ch 12 has the highest density of atmospheric-sound words (6 instances) and two fluorescent passages that explicitly state the atmosphere hasn't changed ('unchanged hum,' 'nothing up here has changed at all'). By Ch 12 (Day 11) the reader has been told the fluorescents hum and nothing changes dozens of times. Manuscript-wide atmospheric sounds: 35 (target 8-10).
findings.rewrite_direction: Cut 4 of the 6 sound-word instances. Remove the explicit 'unchanged'/'nothing has changed' constructions — if the atmosphere is unchanged, omit it rather than narrating its sameness. Keep one atmospheric grounding per major scene shift (the assault prep, the stairwell descent).

findings.acceptance_test: Ch 12 first-floor corridor entry does not repeat the 'forty meters of fluorescent' construction from Ch 2.
findings.evidence: Ch 2 line 302: 'The Floor 1 corridor is forty meters of fluorescent-lit linoleum'; Ch 2 line 402: 'The corridor to the cafeteria is forty meters'; Ch 12 line 2361: 'The first-floor service corridor is forty meters of fluorescent light.'
findings.problem: The first-floor corridor is described as 'forty meters of fluorescent [light/linoleum]' three times. Ch 2 uses the construction twice internally, and Ch 12 reuses it nearly verbatim ten chapters later. The reader knows the corridor's length and lighting.
findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 12 line 2361, drop the 'forty meters of fluorescent light' tag. Enter the corridor through what has changed (the bodies, the smell, Lena's territory) rather than the physical measurements the reader already carries.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter word count is >= 4500 while preserving heading contract.
findings.rewrite_direction: Add causally necessary scene material that strengthens the chapter engine, increases pressure, and lands the planned state shift without filler.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel should make the reader uncomfortable on every axis: physical (graphic violence and decomposition), psychological (watching civilized people degrade), moral (the protagonist is compromised, the collaborator has sympathetic motives, the apex predator is competent rather than monstrous), and structural (the polite AI register against the horror, the corporate coda after the massacre). Lean into every discomfort the premise creates. The worst creative risk is pulling the punch. Ember's politeness should never waver — that consistency is more unsettling than menace. The deaths should never be clean. The protagonist should never be heroic. The ending should offer no comfort, no lesson, no redemption. The stock price jumps eleven percent. That is the last sentence of the novel. Let it land.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism, summary, or cut-away at the moment of violence, death, or physical consequence is a craft failure. This is a horror novel. The deaths must be graphic, ugly, physical, and specific on the page. The reader must be in the room. If a character is beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, the reader hears the sound. If someone dies of chemical burns, the reader sees the skin. If someone bleeds out, the reader watches the eleven minutes. No fading to black, no tasteful elision, no looking away. The bodies stay.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Priya, Chen Wei, and Viktor, who are naturally more formal. Even formal characters should contract under stress. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate precision, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-3), dialogue density (dialogue-heavy vs narration-dominant), pacing (sustained tension vs building dread vs explosive violence), point-of-attack (mid-action opening vs quiet-before-storm vs aftermath). Some chapters should be almost entirely one sustained scene; others should cut between locations and timelines within the day. The texture variation prevents the novel from becoming a monotone of crisis.
prose_style_profile.diction: Clean, precise, contemporary. Not literary-lyrical and not hard-boiled. Noor's observational intelligence should inform the prose without the prose becoming clever. The diction is a window, not a performance. Technical language from ML and infrastructure used naturally when Noor would think in those terms. Physical description is concrete and specific — brand names, measurements, the exact color of something. Never vague when precision is available.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Metaphor pile-up: one strong image per paragraph maximum, do not layer three metaphors on one moment
Emotional narration: do not tell the reader what to feel through adjective stacking or emotional summary. Show the thing and let the feeling happen.
Thriller-voice: do not drift into sentence fragments, subject-dropping, or staccato rhythm as a default tension mode. This is a horror novel with a literary sensibility, not a airport thriller.
Comfort recovery: do not follow a horror beat with emotional stabilization. No character 'takes a breath and feels steadier.' The dread compounds.
Ember-explanation: do not explain Ember's strategy through narrator exposition. It should emerge through Noor's analysis and observation.
Purple death: do not poeticize the deaths. They are ugly, physical, specific. No one dies beautifully.
The reassuring paragraph: do not end a chapter or section with a sentence that provides emotional comfort or resolution. End on the thing that stays.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third, locked to Noor. The reader sees, hears, smells, and knows only what Noor does. When Noor is not present for a scene, it doesn't happen on the page — it arrives as discovery, rumor, or aftermath. The camera never leaves her.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Propulsive and varied. Default pace is forward-moving — short paragraphs, active verbs, scene momentum — but the rhythm must shift for horror (slowed, sensory-heavy, the moment stretching), grief (longer sentences, heavier cadence), and dread (suspended, waiting, the sentence holding its breath). No single rhythm for more than two consecutive pages.
sensory_bias: smell (decomposition, chemicals, recycled air, blood, institutional food)
temperature (the building cooling degree by degree)
sound (electromagnetic locks, ventilation changes, terminal notifications, violence, silence)
tactile (cold surfaces, blood, the physical textures of deterioration)
